root_text.sn56.53:1.1: “Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, yatthimā mahānadiyo saṁsandanti samenti, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.sn56.53:1.2: gaṅgā, yamunā, aciravatī, sarabhū, mahī, tato puriso dve vā tīṇi vā udakaphusitāni uddhareyya.
root_text.sn56.53:1.4: katamaṁ nu kho bahutaraṁ—yāni dve vā tīṇi vā udakaphusitāni ubbhatāni, yaṁ vā sambhejjaudakan”ti?
root_text.sn56.53:1.5: “Etadeva, bhante, bahutaraṁ, yadidaṁ—saṁbhejjaudakaṁ; appamattakāni dve vā tīṇi vā udakaphusitāni ubbhatāni.
root_text.sn56.53:1.6: Saṅkhampi na upenti, upanidhampi na upenti, kalabhāgampi na upenti saṁbhejjaudakaṁ upanidhāya dve vā tīṇi vā udakaphusitāni ubbhatānī”ti.

translation_text.sn56.53:1.1: “Mendicants, there are places where the great rivers—the Ganges, Yamuna, Aciravatī, Sarabhū, and Mahī—join together and converge.
translation_text.sn56.53:1.2: Suppose a person was to draw two or three drops of water from such a place.
translation_text.sn56.53:1.4: Which is more: the two or three drops drawn out or the water in the confluence?”
translation_text.sn56.53:1.5: “Sir, the water in the confluence is certainly more. The two or three drops drawn out are tiny.
translation_text.sn56.53:1.6: Compared to the water in the confluence, it doesn’t count, there’s no comparison, it’s not worth a fraction.”
translation_text.sn56.53:1.7: “In the same way, for a noble disciple …
translation_text.sn56.53:1.8: That’s why you should practice meditation …”
